one_liner: A 19-year-old college student is brought in by his parents for worsening tremor, slurred speech, and declining school performance over the past 6 months. He has also had fatigue and intermittent jaundice.

stage2.hpi_extension: His family has noticed a coarse, 'wing-beating' tremor, progressive clumsiness, drooling, and personality changes with new depression and impulsivity. His grades have plummeted. He developed scleral icterus twice in the past year. He denies alcohol use, IV drugs, or acetaminophen. His older brother died at age 24 of 'liver failure'.
stage2.family_history: Brother died at 24 of fulminant hepatic failure; parents are second cousins (consanguineous).
stage2.social_history: College sophomore. No tobacco, alcohol, or drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: Jaundiced. Eyes: golden-brown rings around the corneal limbus on slit-lamp examination (Kayser-Fleischer rings). Neuro: resting and intention tremor, dysarthria, drooling, and mask-like facies (parkinsonism); mild dystonic posturing of the left hand. Psych: flat affect, depressed mood. Abdomen: soft, liver edge palpable 2 cm below the costal margin, nontender. Spider angiomata on the upper chest.

lab_results.result: Serum ceruloplasmin 8 mg/dL (LOW, normal 20–35). 24-hour urinary copper markedly elevated (>200 µg/24h). Serum free (non-ceruloplasmin-bound) copper elevated. Total serum copper low.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Low ceruloplasmin with elevated urinary copper excretion is the classic biochemical signature of Wilson disease. Ceruloplasmin is an acute phase reactant, so borderline values do not exclude the diagnosis.

lab_results.result: AST 145, ALT 98, total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL, alk phos 38 U/L (LOW — characteristically LOW in Wilson). Albumin 3.0 g/dL, INR 1.6. Hemoglobin 10.5 with haptoglobin low (Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia).
lab_results.clinical_significance: The combination of a transaminitis with an inappropriately LOW alkaline phosphatase and Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia is classic for Wilson disease. Free copper causes hepatocyte injury and RBC hemolysis.

lab_results.result: Liver biopsy: steatosis, periportal inflammation, and early fibrosis. Hepatic copper content markedly elevated (>250 µg/g dry weight). Rhodanine stain positive.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated hepatic copper on quantitative biopsy is the gold-standard confirmatory test when serum studies are equivocal.

stage5.final_clue: MRI brain shows T2 hyperintensity in the basal ganglia ('face of the giant panda' sign in the midbrain). He is started on penicillamine with oral zinc and counseled to avoid copper-rich foods. Genetic testing confirms ATP7B mutations.

debrief.classic_presentation: Wilson disease (hepatolenticular degeneration) classically presents in children and young adults (5–35) with a combination of hepatic dysfunction (chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, fulminant failure), neurologic symptoms (tremor, parkinsonism, dystonia, dysarthria, ataxia), and psychiatric symptoms (depression, personality change, psychosis). Kayser-Fleischer rings are seen in nearly all patients with neurologic involvement. Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia may occur from free-copper–mediated RBC lysis.
debrief.pathophysiology: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the ATP7B gene on chromosome 13, which encodes a hepatocyte copper-transporting ATPase. Loss of function impairs biliary copper excretion and incorporation of copper into ceruloplasmin. Copper accumulates in hepatocytes, causing hepatitis and cirrhosis, then spills into the blood and deposits in the basal ganglia, cornea (Descemet membrane -> Kayser-Fleischer rings), and kidneys (proximal RTA, Fanconi syndrome).
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line treatment is chelation therapy with penicillamine (add pyridoxine/B6 to prevent deficiency) or trientine. Oral zinc induces intestinal metallothionein and blocks copper absorption and is used for maintenance and in presymptomatic patients. Avoid copper-rich foods (liver, shellfish, chocolate, nuts). Liver transplantation is curative and indicated for fulminant failure or decompensated cirrhosis. Screen first-degree relatives.
high_yield_pearls: Wilson disease = autosomal recessive ATP7B mutation -> defective hepatocyte copper excretion -> copper accumulation in liver, brain, and cornea.
Triad: hepatic dysfunction + neurologic (tremor, parkinsonism, dysarthria) + psychiatric symptoms. Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit lamp.
Labs: LOW ceruloplasmin, HIGH urinary copper, LOW serum total copper, HIGH free copper. Alkaline phosphatase is characteristically LOW relative to bilirubin.
Coombs-NEGATIVE hemolytic anemia can occur due to oxidative damage from free copper.
Treat with penicillamine (chelator, give with pyridoxine) or trientine; oral zinc for maintenance. Liver transplant is curative.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Gastrointestinal chapter, 'Hepatic disease — Wilson disease (hepatolenticular degeneration)' section.
